titleStmt.title: Letter from Fr Francis M. Shaw to Fr Provincial Thomas V. Nolan, 14 May 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Francis Shaw

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Fr Francis M. Shaw (1881-1924) to Father Provincial Thomas V. Nolan (1867-1941) about Shaw's travels in Durban. Francis, or Frank, Shaw SJ was born in Ennis, county Clare. He was ordained on 31 July 1916 at Milltown Park, Dublin. Fr Shaw joined the war early in 1916, working in the 16th General Field Hospital at Le Tréporte. In 1917, possibly due to his nationalistic views, Fr Shaw was dispatched to India, and later to Mesopotamia. Fr. Thomas V. Nolan was the Provincial of the Irish Province of the Society of Jesus (1912-1922) and was a member of the Distribution Committee which looked after the welfare and distribution of the Belgian refugees who arrived in Ireland as a consequence of the First World War.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
14 May 1917 Dear Reverend Father Provincial, P. C. You will be wondering where I have
got to. I am still alive, and in the best of form. I have
had a very pleasant stay in Durban. I was two nights
at a camp, but them the O. M. I. Fathers who have the
Naval Vicariate brought me into the Cathedral where I have
been since. They have all, including the Bishop,
Mgr. Delalle, been exceedingly kind to me. One of them
is Irish â Fr James O' Donnell, the rest French â but they
all speak English. I think in case you want to
write to me that a letter addressed c/o Jesuit Fathers
Bombay will reach me sometime or other. At present
Fr Garrold S. J. is staying here. He arrived last night. I have preached several times in the Cathedral so I
will be ready for the Lenten Lectures at Gardiner St.
when the war is over!! I did not send you the cheque
after all. I was not sure of what expenses I would have
before reaching my destination. But I will send something
along first opportunity. I have not said any masses for
your Reverence lately â I should be obliged if you would tell
Fr Farley all the masses I say here are for Church
intentions; and on the ship I would only say Mass half
the time. He will be safe to cancel half March, all April, and
half May, as any Masses I did say on board were also for
intentions. I hope all are well in Ireland. There are several Irish
families here and at the Cape all of them very nice,
and true to the old country. I saw in last nights
paper that the Sinn Feiners have captured another seat
S. Longford. I shall write again as soon as possible. Your Reverences servant in Christ, Francis M. Shaw S. J.
